comment_text.snp4.9:1.1: Māra’s daughters, the archetypal temptresses. Their failed seduction is told at SN 4.25.
comment_text.snp4.9:1.3: The commentary is surely correct in glossing these lines as kimevidaṁ imissā dārikāya muttakarīsapuṇṇaṁ rūpaṁ. Idaṁ (neuter) refers to the body (rūpa) not to the girl. It’s a meaningful distinction; the Buddha was criticizing the nature of the body, not shaming the person.
comment_text.snp4.9:1.4: Icche is 1st optative. Naṁ is rendered as feminine (“her”) by Norman and Bodhi, but it agrees with idaṁ in the previous line, so it should be neuter (“it”). Again, the Buddha says nothing in criticism of the woman, only of the body.
comment_text.snp4.9:3.1: The sense only emerges with the specific nuance of terms. Niccheyya means to “judge, decide” not simply “consider”; and samuggahītaṁ means “[a view that is] adopted” (not “assumed” or “grasped”). Finally, the na here applies, not just to idaṁ vadāmi, but also to samuggahītaṁ, with which idaṁ agrees.
comment_text.snp4.9:4.2: Two Māgaṇḍiyas appear in the Pali canon: here and in MN 75 where he attacks the Buddha as a destroyer of life. As to the protagonist of the current sutta, the Pali commentary, Divyāvadāna 36—which calls him Mākandika—and the Chinese parallel in the Arthapada (T iv 180) say he too hails from the Kuru country, as also at MN 75. Now, the Mahābhārata and other Sanskrit sources name Mākandī as a city or province on the banks of the Ganges, in the Pañcāla kingdom south of Hastināpura. This more-or-less agrees with Māgaṇḍiya’s home in the Kuru country, and suggests his name means “the man from Mākandī”. Divyāvadāna 36 and Sanskrit fragment say the Mākandika of the current sutta was also a wanderer, which does not contradict the position of the Pali commentaries and the Arthapada that he was a brahmin. At AN 5.294–302:1.1 māgaṇḍika is listed as a type of ascetic; these could well be students of Māgaṇḍiya. Kathāsaritsāgara 15 tells the comic farce of an ascetic from Mākandikā on the banks of the Ganges, who fell in love with the daughter of a merchant and went to ridiculous lengths to obtain her, only to end up a laughing-stock. This story could well have begun life as a comic inversion of this sutta. Reading all these sources together, we can posit that there was a brahmin wanderer from Mākandī on the Ganges in Kuru, who gained a certain fame and following as a teacher, but who is mainly remembered for his failed schemes. The Pali commentaries further specify that the Māgaṇḍiya of MN 75 is the nephew of the one in the current sutta.
comment_text.snp4.9:5.1: I think the case should be read here as “instrumental of relation”, since the question was not, “how is peace attained”, but “how is it described”.
comment_text.snp4.9:5.3: Pi here doesn’t qualify sīlabbata (per Norman), it co-ordinates with nopi tena. I capture this with the not/nor construction.
comment_text.snp4.9:10.1: This verse is quoted and discussed at SN 22.3:1.4.
comment_text.snp4.9:12.3: Neyyo has the sense “needing to be led”, for example a student who “requires education” before they can master a passage (AN 4.133:1.3). Here it refers to the arahant.
comment_text.snp4.9:12.4: Anupanīto (“not led in”) relates to neyyo in the previous line. Anupanīto has the sense of “one who has been educated (in recitation)” at MN 93:15.2. Compare English “brought into the fold”. The sense of “dogma” for nivesana is established at Snp 4.3:6.3 and Snp 4.5:6.3.

root_text.snp4.9:6.1: “No ce kira diṭṭhiyā na sutiyā na ñāṇena,
root_text.snp4.9:12.2: Sa mānameti na hi tammayo so;

translation_text.snp4.9:1.1: “Even when I saw the sirens <j>Craving, Malignancy, and Lust,
translation_text.snp4.9:1.2: I had no desire for sex.
translation_text.snp4.9:1.3: What is this body full of piss and shit?
translation_text.snp4.9:1.4: I wouldn’t even want to touch it with my foot.”
translation_text.snp4.9:2.1: “If you do not want a gem such as this,
translation_text.snp4.9:2.2: a lady desired by many rulers of men,
translation_text.snp4.9:2.3: then what manner of theory, <j>precepts and vows, livelihood,
translation_text.snp4.9:2.4: and rebirth in a new life do you assert?”
translation_text.snp4.9:3.3: “none have been adopted thinking, ‘I assert this.’
translation_text.snp4.9:4.1: “O sage, you speak of judgments you have formed,”
translation_text.snp4.9:4.3: “without having adopted any of those views.
translation_text.snp4.9:4.4: As to that matter of ‘inner peace’—
translation_text.snp4.9:4.5: how is that described by the attentive?”
translation_text.snp4.9:5.1: “Purity is spoken of not in terms of view,”
translation_text.snp4.9:5.4: nor in terms of that without view, learning,
translation_text.snp4.9:5.6: Having relinquished these, not adopting them,
translation_text.snp4.9:5.7: peaceful, independent, one would not pray <j>for another life.”
translation_text.snp4.9:6.1: “It seems purity is spoken of not in terms of view,”
translation_text.snp4.9:6.4: nor in terms of that without view, learning,
translation_text.snp4.9:6.6: If so, I think this teaching is sheer folly;
translation_text.snp4.9:6.7: for some believe in purity in terms of view.”
translation_text.snp4.9:7.1: “Continuing to question while relying on a view,”
translation_text.snp4.9:7.3: “you’ve become confused by all you’ve adopted.
translation_text.snp4.9:7.4: From this you’ve not glimpsed the slightest idea,
translation_text.snp4.9:7.5: which is why you consider the teaching folly.
translation_text.snp4.9:8.2: special, or worse’, you’ll dispute about that.
translation_text.snp4.9:8.3: Not wavering due to the three discriminations,
translation_text.snp4.9:8.4: you’ll have no thought ‘I’m equal or special’.
translation_text.snp4.9:9.1: Why would that brahmin say, ‘It’s true’,
translation_text.snp4.9:9.2: or with whom would they dispute, ‘It’s false’?
translation_text.snp4.9:9.3: There is no equal or unequal in them,
translation_text.snp4.9:9.4: so who would they take on in debate?
translation_text.snp4.9:10.1: After leaving their bastion to migrate without abode,
translation_text.snp4.9:10.2: a sage doesn’t get close to anyone in town.
translation_text.snp4.9:10.3: Rid of sensual pleasures, not expecting,
translation_text.snp4.9:10.4: they wouldn’t get in arguments with people.
translation_text.snp4.9:11.1: A spiritual giant would not take up for debate
translation_text.snp4.9:11.2: the things in the world from which they live secluded.
translation_text.snp4.9:11.3: As a prickly lotus born in the water
translation_text.snp4.9:11.4: is unsullied by water and mud,
translation_text.snp4.9:11.5: so the greedless sage, proponent of peace,
translation_text.snp4.9:11.6: is unsmeared by sensuality and the world.
translation_text.snp4.9:12.1: A knowledge master does not become conceited
translation_text.snp4.9:12.2: due to view or thought, <j>for they are not determined by that.
translation_text.snp4.9:12.3: They’ve no need for deeds or learning,
translation_text.snp4.9:13.1: There are no ties for one detached from ideas;
translation_text.snp4.9:13.2: there are no delusions for one freed by wisdom.
translation_text.snp4.9:13.3: But those who have adopted ideas and views

variant_text.snp4.9:2.3: sīlavataṁ nu jīvitaṁ → sīlavatānujīvitaṁ (bj, pts-vp-pli1, mr)
variant_text.snp4.9:12.1: Na vedagū diṭṭhiyāyako → na vedagū diṭṭhiyā (bj, sya-all, pts-vp-pli1)

reference_text.snp4.9:11.1: mc0.501, ms18Sn_1021, ndp20.400, pts-vp-pli166, vnp845, vns852
